prompts: The transformer architecture has become the dominant design pattern for large language models since its introduction in 2017. At its core, the transformer uses a mechanism called self-attention, which allows each position in a sequence to attend to every other position. This is computationally expensive, since the attention matrix scales quadratically with the sequence length. For a sequence of N tokens, the attention computation requires O(N squared) operations and O(N squared) memory for the attention scores. As sequences grow longer, this cost becomes prohibitive. Several techniques have been developed to mitigate this. One approach is sliding window attention, where each token only attends to a fixed window of nearby tokens. This reduces the cost to O(N times W), where W is the window size, at the expense of limiting how far back in the sequence information can flow in a single layer. Another approach is sparse attention, where each token attends only to a carefully chosen subset of other tokens. A third approach, used in models like Mistral and Gemma, is to interleave sliding window and full attention layers, giving the model both local detail and global context. The Gemma family from Google takes this further: in Gemma 4, the first fifteen layers use sliding window attention with a window of 512 tokens and 8 query heads per layer, while a subset of later layers use full causal attention with 16 query heads and a different head dimension. This mixed-head-dim configuration requires careful per-layer dispatch in the inference engine, because every quantity that depends on head count or head dimension must be looked up by layer index rather than read from a global hyperparameter. Even more interesting, Gemma 4 introduces what the authors call per-layer embeddings. Rather than storing a single learned positional embedding or relative position bias, each transformer block receives a small projected representation that is gated into the residual stream after the attention and feedforward stages. The projection chain runs once before the main loop, producing a tensor of shape (per-layer-dim, num-layers, num-tokens), and each block then takes a slice along the layer axis and adds it into its output. This adds a small amount of parameters and a noticeable amount of memory pressure during prefill, since the per-layer tensor must be materialized in the graph allocator for the entire sequence length. On WebGPU backends compiled to WASM, this materialization can exhaust the graph memory pool if it is not sized generously enough. A separate complication arises in cache management. Some Gemma 4 configurations share the key and value cache between certain pairs of layers; this is called shared key-value attention, and it lets the model save roughly half of the cache memory at minimal quality loss. In the inference engine, this means that the cache allocation logic must know which layers own their own cache slot and which layers point into another layer's slot. When the shared layer runs, it must reuse the post-rotary key and pre-permute value tensors that the source layer already computed in the same forward pass. If the source layer has not yet been processed when the shared layer runs, the dispatch breaks and produces garbage outputs. The rotary position embedding itself also has architecture-specific variations. The original RoPE applies a rotation to pairs of dimensions in an interleaved pattern. A later variant, NEOX-style, applies the rotation to the first half and second half of the head dimension instead. Llama and Mistral use the original interleaved pattern; Qwen3 and the Gemma family use the NEOX pattern. Furthermore, Gemma 4 sliding-window layers use a different rotary base frequency than the global layers, and Llama 3.1 ships frequency factors that scale the rotary frequencies per dimension to extend the model's effective context. Getting all of these correct simultaneously requires careful attention in the inference engine to architecture predicates and per-layer scalar lookups. The webllm project tackles all of this in TypeScript orchestration code that runs on top of a patched llama.cpp WebGPU backend compiled to WebAssembly. The TypeScript side handles model loading, tokenization, sampling, and the high-level forward pass orchestration. The WASM side handles the heavy linear algebra and dispatches WebGPU compute shaders. Communication between them is via a small WebAssembly module ABI plus shared HEAPU8 memory for tensors. The split is informed by where the cost lives: TypeScript is fast enough for graph construction and bookkeeping, but the actual matrix multiplications and attention computations need GPU acceleration. Bringing all of this together for a new architecture, like Gemma 4, requires implementing per-layer dispatch for everything that varies by layer, careful integration testing against a known-good reference implementation, and a parity capture pipeline that compares hidden states layer by layer. The parity probe described here is exactly that: it tokenizes a long prompt, runs the model end to end in the browser via a tap-enabled forward pass, captures the intermediate hidden states, and compares them against a HuggingFace transformers reference run on the same prompt. The gate is a per-layer cosine similarity threshold and a top-1 argmax match at the final position. Long prompts exercise the sliding window attention path in a way that short prompts cannot, because only when past plus current tokens exceeds the window size does the banded mask actually differ from the full causal mask. With a window of 512 and a thousand-token prompt, the second half of the sequence is fully in the windowed regime, and any bug in the banded mask construction or per-layer dispatch will surface as a quality cliff at the boundary.
